# Submission

This chapter covers how to submit your work to Gradescope without
losing points to formatting. The same workflow applies to all three
project artifacts you submit: the **Pre-project**, the optional
**Project Progress Check**, and the **Final Project**.

If you only read one section of this chapter, read the
[Saving HTML as PDF](#saving-html-as-pdf) section. The vast majority
of submission errors come from skipping or mangling this step.

## Filename conventions

Each of the three project artifacts has a required filename. Use
**lowercase** for first and last name, **underscores** between
words, and the **`.pdf`** extension.

| Artifact | Filename pattern | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Pre-project | `firstname_lastname_pre.pdf` | `jane_doe_pre.pdf` |
| Project Progress Check | `firstname_lastname_progress.pdf` | `jane_doe_progress.pdf` |
| Final Project | `firstname_lastname_project.pdf` | `jane_doe_project.pdf` |

::: {.callout-important}
Wrong filename = wrong submission = grading script won't find your
file. Triple-check the filename before uploading to Gradescope.
:::

## Late submission policy

Per the syllabus: **late submissions will not be accepted** for the
Pre-project, the Project Progress Check, or the Final Project. The
relevant deadlines are on the course schedule. There are no extensions
and no late-credit options.

## The submission workflow at a glance

Each submission goes through the same three steps:

1. **Knit** your `.Rmd` to HTML inside RStudio (the **Knit** button
   in the editor pane).
2. **Save** the resulting HTML page as a PDF using your browser's
   print-to-PDF function (instructions per browser below).
3. **Upload** the PDF to the corresponding Gradescope assignment
   under the correct filename.

::: {.callout-warning}
## Do not knit directly to PDF

Knitting straight to PDF from R Markdown uses a different rendering
path (LaTeX) that strips the CSS-based slide formatting we use. The
output will not match the reference and will be rejected as a
formatting violation.

You **must** knit to HTML, then save the HTML as PDF.
:::

## Saving HTML as PDF {#saving-html-as-pdf}

After knitting, the HTML output opens in your browser (or in
RStudio's Viewer pane — if it opens there, click the small icon at
the top of the Viewer to open it in your default browser).

In every browser, the print dialog is what you use to save as PDF.
The settings you need are roughly the same across browsers:

- **Destination:** "Save as PDF"
- **Layout / Orientation:** **Landscape**
- **Color:** Color (not Black and White)
- **Margins:** Default or None
- **Pages per sheet:** 1
- **Background graphics:** **Enabled** (this is the easy-to-miss one
  — without it, the colored UGA styling disappears)

Per-browser walkthroughs below.

### Google Chrome (and Microsoft Edge)

Edge uses the same print dialog as Chrome — these steps work for
both.

1. Click the three vertical dots in the top-right of the browser.
2. Click **Print**.
3. Match the settings shown below — most importantly, **Destination
   = Save as PDF**, **Layout = Landscape**, and **More settings →
   Background graphics = checked**.

![Chrome print dialog settings](assets/chrome_print.png){fig-alt="Chrome print dialog with Destination set to Save as PDF, Layout set to Landscape, and Background graphics enabled"}

4. Click **Save**. Use the correct filename when prompted (see
   [Filename conventions](#filename-conventions)).

### Firefox

1. Click the three horizontal lines (the "hamburger" menu) in the
   top-right of the browser.
2. Click **Print**.
3. Match the settings shown below across the two screens. Key
   settings: **Destination = Save to PDF**, **Orientation =
   Landscape**, and **Options → Print backgrounds = checked**.

![Firefox print settings — page 1](assets/firefox_print_1.png){fig-alt="Firefox print dialog with Destination set to Save to PDF and Orientation set to Landscape"}

![Firefox print settings — page 2](assets/firefox_print_2.png){fig-alt="Firefox print dialog showing the Options panel with Print backgrounds enabled"}

4. Click **Save** and use the correct filename.

### Safari

1. Click **File** in the top-left menu bar.
2. Click **Print**.
3. Match the settings shown below across the two screens. Key
   settings: **Orientation = Landscape**, **Print backgrounds =
   checked**, and the **PDF dropdown in the bottom-left** must be
   set to **Save as PDF** (not Print).

![Safari print settings — page 1](assets/safari_print_1.png){fig-alt="Safari print dialog with Orientation set to Landscape and Print backgrounds enabled"}

![Safari print settings — page 2](assets/safari_print_2.png){fig-alt="Safari print dialog with the PDF dropdown set to Save as PDF"}

4. Click **Save** and use the correct filename.

## Pre-project reference: what your three slides should look like

Below are the three pre-project slides rendered correctly. Your
knitted PDF should match these for **formatting** (fonts, colors,
slide layout, code chunk styling). The **content** will differ —
your name will be on the title slide and the numbers in your
print-out and your fill-ins will reflect your `LF.csv`.

::: {.callout-note}
The images below show legacy examples (date stamp from a prior
semester, comma in the name placeholder). They are reference for
the slide *format*, not the exact text content. Your version will
show Summer 2026, your name without a comma, and the current CPS
numbers.
:::

### Reference slide 1 — Title slide

![Title slide reference](assets/ref_slide_1_title.png){fig-alt="Pre-project title slide showing 'BUSN 5000 :: Pre-Project Exercise', subtitle 'Measuring Labor Force Participation', placeholder for student name, and date"}

What yours should look like:

- Title and subtitle as shown
- Your first and last name on the author line
- Summer 2026 date

### Reference slide 2 — Read `LF.csv` and print its contents

![LF print-out slide reference](assets/ref_slide_2_lf.png){fig-alt="Pre-project slide showing the read_csv code chunk and the resulting LF data frame printed in the slide"}

What yours should look like:

- The code chunk visible (formatted with the UGA code-block style)
- The `lf` data frame printed below it with six named columns

### Reference slide 3 — Document the contents of `LF.csv`

![Communication slide reference](assets/ref_slide_3_comm.png){fig-alt="Pre-project slide showing the documentation paragraph about LFPR with all six blanks filled in"}

What yours should look like:

- The paragraph fully filled in, no remaining `_____` blanks
- Values rounded to two decimal places
- Numbers matching your `out/LF.csv` (not numbers copied from this
  reference)

## If your output doesn't match the reference

Most formatting mismatches come from a handful of recurring
mistakes. See the
[Common Errors](common-errors.qmd) chapter for the diagnosis and
fix for each — including:

- CSS in the wrong folder
- Wrong orientation (portrait instead of landscape)
- Knit-to-PDF instead of HTML → PDF
- YAML edits that break the rendering
- Background graphics not enabled in the print dialog

If you've checked all of those and still see a difference, email
the teaching team with your `.Rmd` attached and the print dialog
settings you used.
